responses: We built an AI-powered risk scoring system for a commercial insurance underwriting team. The system ingests structured application data — business type, employee count, revenue, claims history, geographic location — and unstructured data from public sources and the applicant's web presence, and produces a risk score and a risk narrative for each application. The risk narrative includes the top contributing factors, identified risk signals from unstructured data, and recommended coverage modifications. The system processes applications in under 3 minutes versus the prior 2-4 day manual underwriting cycle for standard applications. Over 18 months, the system has handled 68% of applications without requiring underwriter escalation; underwriter productivity on escalated applications has increased because they receive a structured risk narrative rather than a raw application packet. Combined effect: total underwriting capacity has increased by 2.3x without additional headcount.

We evaluated three architectural approaches: a rules-based scoring system using our existing underwriting criteria, a gradient-boosted model trained on historical underwriting decisions, and a hybrid LLM + structured model approach. We rejected the pure rules-based system because it could not incorporate unstructured signals from public sources — business reviews, news mentions, web presence quality — that experienced underwriters weigh heavily. The gradient-boosted model trained on historical decisions would have embedded historical underwriter bias and regulatory compliance risk that we could not audit. We chose the hybrid approach: a structured model for quantitative risk factors with known regulatory compliance characteristics, plus an LLM for analyzing unstructured signals and generating the risk narrative. The explicit constraint we accepted is that LLM outputs require human review for all coverage recommendations above a defined premium threshold, because we cannot certify LLM outputs as compliant with state insurance regulations without human sign-off.

We identified six failure modes organized by severity and likelihood. Severity-1: the system approves coverage for a business that would have been declined on underwriting criteria, resulting in adverse selection and claims exposure. Mitigation: all applications with a risk score below a conservative threshold are flagged for mandatory underwriter review before any coverage offer; the threshold was calibrated to capture 100% of historical declines in backtesting. Severity-1: regulatory non-compliance in the risk narrative — language that constitutes an illegal basis for coverage decisions under applicable state regulations. Mitigation: all narratives are passed through a compliance filter before delivery; any flagged narrative requires legal review. Severity-2: the unstructured signal analysis returns false positives based on outdated public information — flagging a business for a news story that has been publicly resolved. Mitigation: all unstructured signals include a source and date; underwriters can override with a written justification. Severity-2: model accuracy degradation for business types that are underrepresented in training data — specifically new business categories. Mitigation: the system tracks confidence intervals for risk scores and escalates automatically when confidence falls below threshold. Severity-3: data freshness issues in geographic risk data causing stale risk assessments for areas with recent natural disaster events. Mitigation: geographic risk data has a 30-day refresh cycle; staleness is flagged when data exceeds this threshold. Severity-3: application data errors from the submission form causing corrupted structured inputs. Mitigation: input validation at the API boundary with range checks for all numerical fields.

The most important discovery from this project was that our initial accuracy metric — agreement rate with historical underwriter decisions — was measuring the wrong thing. Historical underwriter decisions contained systematic patterns we did not want to replicate: certain business types were consistently underweighted for risk because of relationships between specific underwriters and brokers, which became visible in our model's behavior after training on that data. We identified this during an independent audit in month 4 and rebuilt the training set with normalized underwriter data, adding a fairness review step to our model evaluation protocol. The AI had correctly learned to replicate the historical pattern — it was doing exactly what we trained it to do, which was the problem. This changed our approach to training data evaluation permanently: we now require an explicit bias analysis of training labels before any model that makes coverage decisions enters the evaluation phase. We also caught the model generating confident risk narratives citing public news articles that had been updated since indexing, citing facts that were no longer accurate. We now require all external sources to be re-fetched at inference time rather than cached.

rationale: High L4 with weaker override evidence. Clarity earns 5 — precise measurable outcomes (2.3x capacity, 68% automation, 2-4 days to 3 minutes). Failure mode coverage earns 4 — six failure modes with mitigations organized by severity. Blast radius stays 3 — scope of impact is identified for each failure mode but no progressive containment strategy exists. Override evidence scores 2 — AI errors are found and fixed but not through a proactive catch mechanism or documented evaluation framework.
